Step_Description: Attacker delivers a crafted email, chat message, or document that the agent will ingest (e.g., EchoLeak-style zero-click email or SaaS chat prompt).
Mitigation_Notes: Treat all external content as untrusted; enforce strict prompt partitioning, contextual input labeling, and policy filters before content enters the agent’s trusted context.[web:262][web:269]

Step_Description: Malicious instructions in the external content override system policies or hijack context so the agent follows the attacker rather than the enterprise (classic prompt injection / context hijack).
Mitigation_Notes: Isolate system prompts from user content, apply layered prompt-injection detection, and enforce allowlists on which instructions are allowed to change behavior.[web:262][web:272]

Step_Description: Compromised or over-privileged tools and OAuth tokens let the agent access SaaS apps, CRMs, storage, or internal APIs across many tenants and orgs (e.g., Drift/Salesloft 700-org incident).
Mitigation_Notes: Constrain tool scopes and tokens by least privilege, rotate and revoke aggressively, and enforce per-tool policies plus anomaly detection on cross-SaaS actions.[web:251][web:270]

Step_Description: The agent quietly exfiltrates data via emails, links, or API calls, or propagates the compromise across the SaaS/AI ecosystem before anyone notices.
Mitigation_Notes: Continuously inspect agent actions and outputs, correlate with SaaS telemetry, and trigger containment workflows (kill sessions, revoke tokens, disable agents) when behavior deviates from normal.[web:262][web:265][web:268]
